title: JShell experiments

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for JShell experiments; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Getting Started, Tools, JShell, and Launching Single-File Source-Code Programs; atomic topic: JShell experiments
sources.claim: JShell evaluates snippets in a persistent interactive state that is useful for small API and expression experiments, while saved source remains the reproducible artifact for multi-file programs.

canonicalExplanation.summary: JShell evaluates snippets in a persistent interactive state that is useful for small API and expression experiments, while saved source remains the reproducible artifact for multi-file programs.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The learner evaluates Math.floorDiv(-7, 3) in JShell, records the result, then writes the verified call into a small source file and test. Your goal is to test an expression or API behavior in JShell and transfer it into source code. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; JDK, JVM and Java language. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: JShell evaluates snippets in a persistent interactive state that is useful for small API and expression experiments, while saved source remains the reproducible artifact for multi-file programs. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Snippet declaration order, imports, scratch state, startup configuration, and JShell conveniences can differ from a clean compiled application. The learner must reject this documented misconception: If an expression succeeds once in a long-running JShell session, the same text is automatically a complete reproducible Java program.

includes: Use the REPL for bounded experiments while knowing when a saved program is required.
Test an expression or API behavior in JShell and transfer it into source code.
excludes: Snippet declaration order, imports, scratch state, startup configuration, and JShell conveniences can differ from a clean compiled application.

outcomes: Test an expression or API behavior in JShell and transfer it into source code.

examples.title: JShell experiments: direct language case
examples.situation: The learner evaluates Math.floorDiv(-7, 3) in JShell, records the result, then writes the verified call into a small source file and test.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: The learner evaluates Math.floorDiv(-7, 3) in JShell, records the result, then writes the verified call into a small source file and test.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use JShell for a bounded experiment, then transfer the finding into saved source with explicit imports, declarations, inputs, and a repeatable check.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use JShell for a bounded experiment, then transfer the finding into saved source with explicit imports, declarations, inputs, and a repeatable check.

examples.title: JShell experiments: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A snippet works only because an earlier session imported a package, so a clean reset exposes the missing dependency before the idea moves into source code.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A snippet works only because an earlier session imported a package, so a clean reset exposes the missing dependency before the idea moves into source code.
Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Snippet declaration order, imports, scratch state, startup configuration, and JShell conveniences can differ from a clean compiled application.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: If an expression succeeds once in a long-running JShell session, the same text is automatically a complete reproducible Java program.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Test an expression or API behavior in JShell and transfer it into source code.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If an expression succeeds once in a long-running JShell session, the same text is automatically a complete reproducible Java program.
misconceptions.correction: Use JShell for a bounded experiment, then transfer the finding into saved source with explicit imports, declarations, inputs, and a repeatable check.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: If an expression succeeds once in a long-running JShell session, the same text is automatically a complete reproducible Java program.

analogy.example: a workshop that turns a checked design into a labeled machine part and then runs it on a compatible line can introduce JShell experiments, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: separating source rules, build tools, class files, and runtime execution
limitations: Java compatibility, verification, class loading, and diagnostics follow formal specifications rather than physical manufacturing

recap.summary: You should now be able to test an expression or API behavior in JShell and transfer it into source code. Keep this boundary in view: Snippet declaration order, imports, scratch state, startup configuration, and JShell conveniences can differ from a clean compiled application.
